topic: One Option (Abnormal / Developmental / Health / Human Relationships)
subject: psychology

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: The Robbers Cave study was conducted by:
items.why: It's Sherif. “Asch” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Buss (1989) surveyed how many cultures on mate preferences?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 37. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Hazan and Shaver publish adult attachment styles?
items.why: The real figure is 1987 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
